title: The useContext Hook
summary: Share data across a whole component tree without threading props through every level — the fix for prop-drilling.

blocks.md: When a value (the theme, the current user, a language) is needed by many components at different depths, passing it down as props through every layer — **prop-drilling** — gets painful. **Context** lets a parent broadcast a value to any descendant that asks for it.

blocks.caption: create · provide · consume

blocks.md: `ThemedButton` reads `theme` directly with `useContext`, even though `Toolbar` never received or passed it. Any component inside the Provider can grab the value.

blocks.title: Context isn't a state manager
blocks.md: Context handles *distribution*, not *storage*. You still hold the value in `useState`/`useReducer` and pass it (and its setters) as the Provider's `value`. Also, every consumer re-renders when the Provider's value changes — so don't put rapidly-changing, unrelated values in one big context.

blocks.question: What problem does Context primarily solve?
options: Passing data to deeply nested components without prop-drilling through every level
blocks.explanation: Context lets a Provider share a value with any descendant via useContext, avoiding the need to thread props through intermediate components that don't use them (prop-drilling).

keyTakeaways: Context shares a value with a whole subtree without prop-drilling.
Three steps: createContext, wrap with <Context.Provider value={...}>, read with useContext.
Any descendant inside the Provider can consume the value at any depth.
Context distributes data; you still store it in state. Consumers re-render when the value changes.

exercise.prompt: You have a `user` object in <App> that a deeply nested <Avatar> needs. Outline how to deliver it with Context instead of passing `user` through every component in between.
exercise.hint: createContext, provide the user at the top, useContext in Avatar.
